text: This article examines the rapid expansion of international education as a commercial export, focusing specifically on the Australian model. In recent decades, higher education has shifted from a perceived public good to a private commodity, a transition accelerated by the General Agreement on Trade in Services (GATS). In Australia, this has resulted in an extraordinary "export boom" where education now ranks as a top national export. However, the author argues that this success is precariously built upon the systematic public underfunding of domestic institutions. As universities become increasingly reliant on international tuition fees to subsidize research and basic operations, systemic risks emerge, including the potential erosion of academic quality, increased student-to-faculty ratios, and a neglect of intercultural pedagogy in favor of revenue maximization. The paper concludes that Australia faces a looming dilemma: continue down a path of "dangerous dependence" on commercial recruitment or commit to a public reinvestment strategy to safeguard the long-term integrity and global standing of its higher education sector.
